claim: According to Fig. 2, ferrite forms first during solidification, then austenite via peritectic reaction; with decreasing temperature, ferrite fraction decreases and austenite fraction increases, reaching minimum ferrite around 1000°C.
evidence_text: According to Fig. 2, ferrite is expected to form first during solidification, and then austenite forms by peritectic reaction [33]. After solidification, the phase fraction of ferrite will decrease and that of austenite will increase with the decrease of temperature until the temperature reaches around 1000°C, where the fraction of ferrite is lowest.

claim: Below 823°C, κ-carbide appears by eutectoid reaction γ → α+κ according to the equilibrium phase diagram (Fig. 2).
evidence_text: the phase fractions do not change much until temperature becomes lower than 823°C, where κ-carbide appears by eutectoid reaction γ → α+κ.

claim: XRD (Fig. 4) detects κ-carbide peaks in specimens annealed at 700°C, 800°C, and 850°C, and no peaks of γ phase at 700°C; κ peaks are absent in hot-rolled specimen.
evidence_text: The peaks of κ-carbide are seen in specimen annealed at 700°C, 800°C and 850°C, and no obvious peaks of γ phase can be seen in the specimen annealed at 700°C. In the hot rolled specimen, peaks of κ-carbide are not seen, probably due to the low volume fraction of κ-carbide, or the coherency of κ-carbide and α matrix [36].

claim: OM micrographs (Fig. 5) show κ-carbide dissolves between 850°C and 900°C, with no obvious κ-carbide at 900°C.
evidence_text: When annealed at 900°C, as shown in Fig. 5(f), no obvious κ-carbide can be seen, that is to say, κ-carbide has almost dissolved into α matrix and γ matrix. So, the actual temperature for κ-carbide to dissolve is between 850°C and 900°C.

claim: After annealing at 700°C, austenite decomposes into lamellar κ-carbide and α matrix via eutectoid reaction; TEM reveals N-W orientation relationship between κ-carbide and α, but not all κ-carbide exhibits this relationship (Fig. 6a, Fig. 7).
evidence_text: When annealed at 700°C ... γ phase has almost decomposed to lamellar κ-carbide and α matrix by the eutectoid reaction γ → α+κ. ... Fig. 7(b) is the selected area diffraction pattern and of the lamellar κ-carbide and α matrix, together with its index, which indicates the Nishiyama–Wasserman (N-W) orientation relationship between κ-carbide and α matrix. ... However, not all the κ-carbide has orientation relationship with α matrix

claim: At 800°C, κ-carbide becomes larger, lower in fraction, and more globular compared to 700°C, and no orientation relationship is found with α phase (Fig. 6b, Fig. 8).
evidence_text: Fig. 6(b) is the SEM micrograph of the specimen annealed at 800°C, and compared with the κ-carbide in the specimen annealed at 700°C (Fig. 6(a)), the size of κ-carbide is larger, the fraction of κ-carbide is lower, and the shape is closer to globular. ... Several areas are examined and no orientation relationship is found between κ-carbide and its neighboring α phase.

claim: In the 900–1200°C range, α2 (banded coarse ferrite) and γ grain sizes increase rapidly above 900°C, whereas α1 (ferrite among γ grains) grain size increases rapidly only above 1000°C (Fig. 9).
evidence_text: The grain size of α2 which continuously distributed and the grain size of γ phase increase quickly with the rising of annealing temperature higher than 900°C, while the grain size of α1 which is mainly distributed among γ grains does not increase quickly with the rising of annealing temperature until the temperature is over 1000°C.

claim: Boundary κ-carbide is observed along α/α boundaries in specimens annealed at 900–1200°C, forming during quenching; TEM confirms N-W orientation relationship with α at 1200°C, but large misorientation exists between neighboring κ particles (Fig. 10, Fig. 12).
evidence_text: κ-carbide is indeed found along some boundaries between large α grains in α area in the annealing temperature range 900°C to 1200°C, as shown in Fig. 10(a) through (d). ... The boundary κ-carbide is believed to form during the quenching when annealing temperature is over the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide, which is between 850°C and 900°C. ... Fig. 12(b) shows the N–W orientation relationship between κ-carbide and α matrix. However, the (200)κ DF micrograph ... shows only part of the boundary κ-carbide, which indicates the large misorientation between the neighboring κ-carbide particles.

claim: EDS line scan of 800°C annealed specimen shows α phase has higher Mn and lower Al than γ phase, and κ-carbide has the highest Mn and Al contents (Fig. 13a).
evidence_text: In the specimen annealed at 800°C, Mn content is higher while Al content is lower in α phase compared with those in γ phase, and Mn and Al content in κ-carbide is the highest, higher than those in α phase or γ phase.

claim: XRD measurements (Fig. 14) show no γ phase at 700°C, no κ-carbide at 900°C; γ fraction increases with temperature up to ~60% at 900°C, then varies between 57% and 63% in 900–1200°C, peaking around 1000°C.
evidence_text: The volume fraction change of each phase with the temperature, which is determined from the XRD measurement results, is shown in Fig. 14. ... No γ phase is detected when annealing temperature is 700°C, and no κ-carbide is detected when the annealing temperature is 900°C. ... At 900°C, the γ phase fraction is about 60%. ... the volume fraction of γ phase changes only in the small range of 57% to 63% when the annealing temperature is between 900°C and 1200°C.

claim: TEM SADP analysis reveals no obvious superlattice spots in α or γ phases in all annealed specimens (700–1200°C, 1 h), indicating no significant ordering develops.
evidence_text: TEM analysis (not shown here) indicates that no obvious superlattice spots can be seen in the SADPs of α phase or γ phase in all the annealed specimens, and although some weak superlattice spots can be seen in the SADPs of some grains; they are all too weak to obtain the DF images. So ordering of atoms in α phase or γ phase is not expected during annealing at the temperature range 700–1200°C for 1 h.

claim: The experimentally determined dissolution temperature of κ-carbide is between 850 °C and 900 °C, higher than the 823 °C calculated by Thermo-Calc.
evidence_text: As has been obtained from the OM micrographs in Fig. 5, the dissolution temperature for κ-carbide is between 850 °C and 900 °C, which is higher than that (823 °C) calculated by Thermo-Calc (Fig. 1).

claim: No γ phase is detected when annealing temperature is 700 °C, and no κ-carbide is detected when annealing temperature is 900 °C.
evidence_text: No γ phase is detected when annealing temperature is 700 °C, and no κ-carbide is detected when the annealing temperature is 900 °C.

claim: When annealing temperature increases from 700 °C to 900 °C, the volume fractions of α phase and κ-carbide decrease, and γ phase increases, reaching about 60% at 900 °C.
evidence_text: When the anneal temperature changes from 700 °C to 900 °C, the volume fractions of α phase and κ-carbide decrease, and the fraction of γ phase increases. At 900 °C, the γ phase fraction is about 60%.

claim: After κ-carbide dissolution, the γ volume fraction varies only in the small range of 57% to 63% for annealing temperatures between 900 °C and 1200 °C, reaching its maximum near 1000 °C.
evidence_text: After the dissolution of κ-carbide, the volume fraction of γ phase first increase slightly and then decrease slightly with the increment of annealing temperature, and the fraction of γ phase reaches largest around the annealing temperature of 1000 °C. The volume fraction of γ phase changes only in the small range of 57% to 63% when the annealing temperature is between 900 °C and 1200 °C.

claim: The boundary κ-carbide observed in Fig. 10 cannot be detected by XRD because its volume fraction is too small.
evidence_text: The boundary κ-carbide that was seen in Fig. 10 cannot be detected by XRD, probably due to that the fraction of boundary κ-carbide is too small.

claim: No obvious superlattice spots are observed in SADPs of α or γ phase in any annealed specimen, indicating that atom ordering does not occur during annealing at 700–1200 °C for 1 h; ordering may start during quenching but the time is too short for full development.
evidence_text: In addition, TEM analysis (not shown here) indicates that no obvious superlattice spots can be seen in the SADPs of α phase or γ phase in all the annealed specimens, and although some weak superlattice spots can be seen in the SADPs of some grains; they are all too weak to obtain the DF images. So ordering of atoms in α phase or γ phase is not expected during annealing at the temperature range 700–1200 °C for 1 h, while ordering may occur during quenching, whereas the time is too short for the ordering to well develop.

claim: TEM of the 700 °C annealed specimen reveals globular or short-rod like κ-carbide dispersed in the α matrix, with SADP indexed along [110]κ.
evidence_text: Fig. 7. TEM micrographs of specimen annealed at 700 °C; (a) BF image, (b) SADP of (a) from [110]κ and its index, (c) (200)κ DF image of (a), and (d) BF image of globular or short-rod like κ dispersed in α matrix.

claim: All annealed specimens show continuous yielding except the specimen annealed at 700 °C, for which the work hardening rate curve is calculated only after the yielding period.
evidence_text: The annealed specimens show continuous yielding phenomenon except the specimen annealed at 700 °C. For the specimen annealed at 700 °C, the work hardening rate curve is calculated only after the yielding period.

claim: For specimens annealed at 700 °C, 800 °C and 850 °C with κ-carbide in the matrix, the work hardening rates are in the reverse order of the annealing temperatures when true strain is less than about 5%.
evidence_text: For the specimens annealed at 700 °C, 800 °C and 850 °C, with κ-carbide distributed in the α or γ matrix, the work hardening rates of the specimens are in the reverse order of the annealing temperatures when true strain is less than about 5%.

claim: Specimens annealed at or above 900 °C, without obvious κ-carbide in the matrix, exhibit lower work hardening rates than specimens annealed at 700–850 °C when true strain is less than about 5%.
evidence_text: While for the specimens annealed at or over 900 °C, with no obvious κ-carbide in the α or γ matrix, the work hardening rates are lower than those of the specimens annealed at 700 °C, 800 °C or 850 °C when true strain is less than about 5%.

claim: The high early-stage work hardening rates of specimens annealed at 700–850 °C decrease quickly until failure, while specimens annealed at 900 °C and 950 °C possess similar work hardening rates but the 950 °C specimen snapped earlier.
evidence_text: Although the specimens annealed at 700 °C, 800 °C, and 850 °C bear a high work hardening rate at the early stage, their work hardening rates decrease quickly until failure occurs. For specimens annealed at 900 °C and 950 °C, they possess similar work hardening rate, while specimen annealed at 950 °C snapped earlier.

claim: After annealing at 900 °C for 1 h, the steel exhibits an ultimate tensile strength of 855 MPa, yield strength of 651 MPa, ultimate elongation of 36.6%, and a product of strength and ductility of 31 GPa%.
evidence_text: After annealed at 900 °C for 1 h, the ultimate strength, yield strength, ultimate elongation, and the product of strength and ductility, are 855 MPa, 651 MPa, 36.6%, 31 GPa%, respectively.

claim: Ultimate strength and yield strength generally decrease with increasing annealing temperature, while ultimate elongation first increases from 700 °C to 900 °C and then decreases from 900 °C to 1200 °C, with a very violent change in the 800–1000 °C range; the product of strength and ductility is mainly controlled by elongation.
evidence_text: the ultimate strength and yield strength have the general trend of decreasing with the increase of the annealing temperature, and the ultimate elongation first increase when the annealing temperature rises from 700 °C to 900 °C and then decrease with the temperature rises from 900 °C to 1200 °C. The change of ultimate elongation is very violent especially in the annealing temperature range 800 °C to 1000 °C, and the change of the product of strength and ductility is mainly decided by the change of ultimate elongation.

claim: Large secondary cracks are well developed in all specimens and are believed to be related to the banded microstructure.
evidence_text: Large secondary cracks are well developed in all the specimens, according to Fig. 17(a), (c), and (e), which shows the fractographs at low magnification of the specimen annealed at 800 °C, 900 °C, and 1000 °C, respectively. The large secondary cracks are believed to be related with the banded microstructure.

claim: Fracture at 800 °C proceeds by a mixture of ductile transgranular fracture of γ (dimpled area) and quasi-cleavage of α (river pattern), with κ-carbide distributed in the matrix and along grain boundaries contributing to cleavage fracture from boundaries.
evidence_text: The fracture in the specimen annealed at 800 °C takes place by a mixture mode of ductile fracture and quasi-cleavage fracture. The dimpled area is mainly produced by the ductile transgranular fracture of γ, and the river pattern is mainly produced by the quasi-cleavage of α phase. The κ-carbide distributed in the matrix and along the grain boundaries is sure to contribute to the cleavage fracture from grain boundaries.

claim: At 900 °C and 1000 °C, fracture is predominantly ductile fracture of γ mixed with quasi-cleavage of α, but the ductile fracture area is more obvious at 900 °C than at 1000 °C.
evidence_text: For the specimens annealed at 900 °C and at 1000 °C, the fracture mode is predominant by the mixture of the ductile fracture of γ phase and the quasi-cleavage fracture of α phase. However, the ductile fracture area in specimen annealed at 900 °C is more obvious than that in specimen annealed at 1000 °C.

claim: The effect of boundary κ-carbide along large α grains on the fracture of the 900 °C and 1000 °C annealed specimens cannot be determined from the fractographs in Fig. 17(c) through (e).
evidence_text: The effect of the boundary κ-carbide along some large α grains on the fracture of specimen annealed at 900 °C and 1000 °C cannot be obtained from Fig. 17(c) through (e).

claim: The specimen annealed at 900°C exhibits a more obvious ductile fracture area than the specimen annealed at 1000°C, but the effect of boundary κ-carbide on fracture could not be determined from Fig. 17(c)-(e).
evidence_text: However, the ductile fracture area in specimen annealed at 900 °C (Fig. 17(d)) is more obvious than that in specimen annealed at 1000 °C. The effect of the boundary κ-carbide along some large α grains on the fracture of specimen annealed at 900 °C and 1000 °C cannot be obtained from Fig. 17(c) through (e).

claim: After annealing for 1 h between 700 °C and 1200 °C, the steel is composed of ferrite (α), austenite (γ) and κ-carbide, and the banded structure still exists after the annealing.
evidence_text: The microstructures of the steel after annealing for 1 h at the temperature range 700 °C to 1200 °C in the present study are basically composed of ferrite (α), austenite (γ) and κ-carbide, and the banded structure still exists after the annealing.

claim: After annealing at 700 °C for 1 h, the γ phase has almost totally decomposed to α phase and κ-carbide by the eutectoid reaction γ→α+κ, and the N-W orientation relationship is found mainly between some lamellar κ-carbide and the α matrix (Fig. 7).
evidence_text: The γ phase has almost totally decomposed to α phase and κ- carbide by eutectoid reaction γ→α+κ after annealing at 700 °C for 1 h, and the N–W orientation relationship is found mainly between some lamellar κ-carbide and the α matrix (Fig. 7).

claim: At 800 °C and 850 °C, the γ volume fraction is about 29% and 39% respectively; from 700 °C to 900 °C, κ-carbide and α fraction decrease while γ fraction increases, and the N-W orientation relationship between κ and α is hardly found at 800 °C (Fig. 8).
evidence_text: When annealed at 800 °C or 850 °C, the γ phase volume fraction is about 29% and 39%, respectively. The κ-carbide and α phase fraction decreases, while the γ phase fraction increases with annealing temperature rising from 700 °C to 900 °C. The N–W orientation relationship has been hardly found between κ-carbide and α matrix when annealing temperature reaches 800 °C (Fig. 8).

claim: With increasing annealing temperature, κ-carbide at or near α/γ boundaries dissolves first, while κ along α/α boundaries of large grains dissolves last, indicating the dissolution temperature of κ along α/α boundary is higher than that along α/γ boundary; this is attributed to slightly higher Al content in κ at α/α boundaries and the strong effect of Al on raising the κ solvus.
evidence_text: With the increase of annealing temperature, the κ-carbide distributed at or near the boundary of α phase and γ phase dissolves first and the κ-carbide along α/α boundaries of large grains dissolves last. It indicates that the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide along α/α boundary is higher than that along α/γ boundary in the present steel... This may mainly come from that the Al content in the κ-carbide at α/α boundary is slightly higher than that at α/γ boundary due to the nonuniform of the microstructure, and it has been pointed out that the effect of Al on raising the solvus of κ-carbide is larger in austenitic Fe–Mn–Al–C steels [42].

claim: For 1 h annealing, the actual dissolution temperature of κ lies between 850 °C and 900 °C, while Thermo-Calc predicts about 823 °C; the discrepancy is attributed to thermodynamic calculation error and the non-equilibrium condition.
evidence_text: When the annealing time is 1 h, the actual dissolution temperature for κ is between 850 °C and 900 °C, while the dissolution temperature of κ in the equilibrium phase diagram calculated by Thermo-Calc is about 823 °C, which is lower. This may be attributed to not only the error of the thermodynamic calculation, but also the difference between equilibrium and disequilibrium system.

claim: When annealing temperature exceeds 900 °C, the γ phase fraction changes moderately: from about 60% at 900 °C to about 63% at 1000 °C, then decreases to about 57% at 1200 °C (Fig. 14).
evidence_text: When the annealing temperature is over 900 °C, the fraction of γ phase change moderately, first increases from about 60% at 900 °C to about 63% at 1000 °C, and then decreases to about 57% at 1200 °C (Fig. 14).

claim: In specimens annealed at ≥900 °C, boundary κ-carbide forms during quenching; with increasing temperature, Mn, C, and Al segregate to boundaries to produce κ; in the 1200 °C specimen, needle-like or plate-like FCC particles appear in large α grains and compete for Mn and C with boundary κ, tending to disappear near grain boundaries (Figs. 10, 11, 12).
evidence_text: The boundary κ-carbide in the specimens annealed at the temperature of or over 900 °C (Figs. 10, 11 and 12) is thought to form during quenching. With the increase of temperature, the Mn and C elements, together with Al element in the α phase with large grains segregate to the boundary and form κ-carbide. In some large α grains in the specimen annealed at 1200 °C, the needle-like or plate-like FCC particles (Fig. 10(d)), are thought to come from the quick transformation from BCC to FCC. It is easy to know that the needle-like or plate-like particles are competing for Mn or C elements with boundary κ-carbide, since the needle-like or plate-like particles tend to disappear when becoming close to grain boundary.

claim: The α1 grain size decreases from 3.2 μm to 2.5 μm when annealing temperature rises from 900 °C to 950 °C, mainly because the α phase fraction decreases slightly and the movement of the α/γ phase boundary toward the α side affects the small α1 grains significantly.
evidence_text: While the grain size of α1 even decreases from 3.2 μm to 2.5 μm when annealing temperature rises from 900 °C to 950 °C... When the annealing temperature rises from 900 °C to 950 °C, the α phase fraction decreases slightly (Fig. 14), and the movement of α/γ phase boundary toward α phase side has a more significant effect on the grain size of α1

claim: Unlike Fe-20Mn-9Al-0.6C steel where γ grain growth is more affected by temperature, the present steel shows no clear difference in temperature effect on grain growth between α2 and γ; α1 grows slower than γ from 950 to 1100 °C and then at a similar speed above 1100 °C.
evidence_text: In the steel Fe–20Mn–9Al–0.6C studied by Hwang et al. [25], the temperature effect on the grain growth is larger in γ phase than α phase, however, in the present steel, this is something different when α phase is classified into α1 ... the difference of temperature effect on the grain growth of α2 and γ phase is hard to tell. While for α1, the grain size ... then increase slower than γ phase until annealing temperature reaches 1100 °C, and then increase at the similar speed with γ phase.

claim: Grain size evolution during annealing is affected not only by recovery, recrystallization, and grain growth, but also by phase transformation and the movement of phase boundaries.
evidence_text: The grain size is affected not only by recovery, recrystallization, and growth after recrystallization, but also by the phase transformation and the movement of phase boundary.

claim: When the annealing temperature increases from 900 °C to 950 °C, the α phase fraction decreases slightly, and the movement of the α/γ phase boundary toward the α phase side significantly reduces the size of α1 grains distributed among γ grains, as shown in Fig. 6(d) and (e).
evidence_text: When the annealing temperature rises from 900 °C to 950 °C, the α phase fraction decreases slightly (Fig. 14), and the movement of α/γ phase boundary toward α phase side has a more significant effect on the grain size of α1, which is mainly distributed among γ grains and has a small initial grain size.

claim: The ultimate tensile strength and yield strength of the steel decrease with increasing annealing temperature, while the ultimate elongation first increases up to 900 °C and then decreases above 900 °C; the product of strength and ductility follows the same trend as ultimate elongation (Fig. 16).
evidence_text: The ultimate tensile strength and yield strength of the steel in the present study has a trend of decrease with the increment of annealing temperature, and the ultimate elongation first increase with the increment of annealing temperature when temperature is lower than 900 °C and then decrease when annealing temperature is over 900 °C (Fig. 16), and the trend of the product of strength and ductility of the annealed specimens against temperature resembles the trend of ultimate elongation.

claim: A yield phenomenon, typical of ferritic steels, only appears in the specimen annealed at 700 °C.
evidence_text: Yield phenomenon, which is usually seen in the ferrite steels, only appears in the specimen annealed at 700 °C.

claim: In the specimen annealed at 700 °C for 1 h, the γ phase has almost totally transformed into α phase and κ-carbide via eutectoid reaction; the microstructure consists of large α grains, continuous κ-carbide along α grain boundaries, and areas with lamellar κ within the γ matrix (Fig. 6(a)).
evidence_text: In the specimen annealed at 700 °C, the γ phase has almost totally transformed into α phase and κ-carbide, and the microstructure is composed of large α grains, continuous κ-carbide along α grain boundaries, and areas containing lamellar κ-carbide within γ matrix (Fig. 6(a)).

claim: After annealing at 700 °C for 1 h, the specimen shows an ultimate tensile strength of 971 MPa, yield strength of 837 MPa, ultimate elongation of 4.8%, and product of strength and ductility of 5 GPa% (Table 1).

For the 800 °C annealed specimen, the κ-carbide fraction decreases and its size coarsens compared to 700 °C; continuous κ-carbide along large α grains remains, while the presence of γ phase improves ductility, resulting in decreased strength and slightly increased elongation (Fig. 6(b), Fig. 16).
evidence_text: When annealing temperature is 800 °C, the microstructure is composed of α phase, γ phase and κ-carbide (Fig. 6(b)). Compared with the specimen annealed at 700 °C (Fig. 6(a)), the fraction of κ-carbide has decreased, and the size of κ-carbide has coarsened, which indicates that the strengthening and ductility from κ-carbide will be weakened in the specimen annealed at 800 °C. The κ-carbide still continuously distributed along the large α grains will decrease the ductility. However, the γ phase in the microstructure can increase the ductility.

claim: After annealing at 800 °C for 1 h, the specimen exhibits an ultimate tensile strength of 877 MPa, yield strength of 702 MPa, ultimate elongation of 7.8%, and product of strength and ductility of 7 GPa% (Table 1).
The rapid increase in ductility for the 850 °C annealed specimen is mainly attributed to the increase of γ phase fraction.
evidence_text: For the specimen annealed at 850 °C, the quick increase of the ductility may mainly attributed to the increase of γ phase fraction.

claim: In the 850 °C specimen, κ-carbide acts as a strong obstacle to deformation, leading to a high initial work hardening rate; however, the lower γ fraction and easier microcrack initiation around κ-carbide cause the work hardening rate to decrease quickly and reduce the ultimate elongation (Fig. 15(b)).
evidence_text: in the specimen annealed at 850 °C, the κ-carbide is harder to deform than α phase and γ phase, and serves as strong obstacle for deformation, leading to a high work hardening rate in the early stage, while the γ phase fraction is lower, and microcracks will also be easier to initiated around the κ-carbide, making the work hardening rate decrease more quickly, and causing the reduction of ultimate elongation in the later stage.

claim: After annealing at 850 °C for 1 h, the specimen shows an ultimate tensile strength of 879 MPa, yield strength of 693 MPa, ultimate elongation of 21.7%, and product of strength and ductility of 19 GPa% (Table 1).

At 900 °C, κ-carbide dissolves during annealing; only small boundary κ-carbide forms during quenching and distributes along some large α grains, and the specimen achieves the best ductility of all annealed conditions with an ultimate elongation of 36.6% (Figs. 6(d) and 10(a)).
evidence_text: When the annealing temperature reaches 900 °C, κ-carbide dissolves during annealing, and there is only some boundary κ-carbide of very small size formed during quenching and distributed along some large α grains (Figs. 6(d) and 10(a)). The ultimate elongation is 36.6%, and the ductility of the specimen is the best of all the annealed specimens.

claim: After annealing at 900 °C for 1 h, the specimen shows an ultimate tensile strength of 855 MPa, yield strength of 651 MPa, ultimate elongation of 36.6%, and product of strength and ductility of 31 GPa% (Table 1).

When annealing temperature exceeds 900 °C, the phase fractions of α and γ change only in a narrow range, and the continuous decrease in tensile strength and ductility is mainly attributed to the increase in average grain size of α and γ phases (Fig. 9), following the Hall–Petch relation.
evidence_text: When the annealing temperature is over 900 °C, the ultimate strength and ultimate tensile strength continuously decrease with the increase of annealing temperature (Fig. 16(a)). The phase fractions of α phase and γ phase change only a little in a narrow range (Fig. 14), which should not be the main reason to cause such a large change in the ultimate strength and ductility. ... The average grain size changes of α phase and γ phase shown in Fig. 9 are believed to be the main reason for the change of tensile properties when annealing temperature is over 900 °C.

claim: After annealing at 950 °C for 1 h, the specimen exhibits an ultimate tensile strength of 804 MPa, yield strength of 581 MPa, ultimate elongation of 26.6%, and product of strength and ductility of 21 GPa% (Table 1).

After annealing at 1000 °C for 1 h, the specimen shows an ultimate tensile strength of 763 MPa, yield strength of 557 MPa, ultimate elongation of 16.2%, and product of strength and ductility of 12 GPa% (Table 1).
SEM fractographs of the 1000 °C annealed specimen (Fig. 17(e) and (f)) show that the ductile fracture area has lessened and is not apparent compared to lower temperatures.
evidence_text: The SEM fractograph (Fig. 17(e) and(f)) shows that the ductile fracture area has already lessened and becomes not apparent in the specimen annealed at 1000 °C.

claim: After annealing at 1100 °C for 1 h, the specimen exhibits an ultimate tensile strength of 671 MPa, yield strength of 518 MPa, ultimate elongation of 12%, and product of strength and ductility of 8 GPa% (Table 1).

After annealing at 1200 °C for 1 h, the specimen shows an ultimate tensile strength of 611 MPa, yield strength of 495 MPa, ultimate elongation of 4.8%, and product of strength and ductility of 3 GPa% (Table 1).
In the 1200 °C annealed specimen, needle-like or plate-like particles in some large α grains undoubtedly promote cracking (Figs. 6(h) and 10(d)).
evidence_text: in the specimen annealed at 1200 °C (Figs. 6(h) and 10(d)), the needle-like or plate-like particles in some large α grains will undoubtedly promote cracking.

claim: The best tensile properties are achieved after annealing at 900 °C for 1 h: ultimate tensile strength 855 MPa, yield strength 651 MPa, ultimate elongation 36.6%, and product of strength and ductility 31 GPa%.
evidence_text: The relatively good tensile properties of the present steel can be obtained after annealing at 900 °C for 1 h, when the ultimate strength, yield strength, ultimate elongation, and the product of strength of strength and ductility, are 855 MPa, 651 MPa, 36.6%, and 31 GPa%, respectively.

claim: The density of the steel is 6.80 g/cm³, which is about 13% lower than that of pure iron.
evidence_text: The density of the steel is 6.80 g/cm3, about 13% lower than that of pure iron.

claim: The phases in the annealed steel are ferrite (α), austenite (γ), and κ-carbide (κ); κ-carbide dissolves between 850 °C and 900 °C; the γ phase fraction first increases, reaching a maximum of about 63% at around 1000 °C, then decreases slightly; boundary κ-carbide formed during quenching exists in all specimens annealed between 900 °C and 1200 °C; α and γ grains grow quickly above 900 °C.
evidence_text: The phases presented in the annealed steel mainly include ferrite (α), austenite (γ) and κ-carbide (κ). The dissolution temperature of κ-carbide is between 850 °C and 900 °C. ... With the increase of temperature, the γ phase fraction first increases, and reaches largest, about 63% at around 1000 °C, and then decreases slightly. The boundary κ-carbide formed during quenching exists in all the specimens annealed at the range 900 °C to 1200 °C. Generally, the α grains and γ grains grow up quickly when the annealing temperature is over 900 °C.

claim: Between 700 °C and 900 °C, ductility is mainly controlled by the fraction of γ phase, while κ-carbide contributes to strength but microcracks preferentially initiate around κ-carbide, impairing tensile properties; between 900 °C and 1200 °C, phase fraction changes are negligible, and grain size becomes the dominant factor controlling strength and ductility.
evidence_text: At the annealing temperature between 700 °C and 900 °C, the ductility of the specimens is mainly controlled by the fraction of γ phase. Although the κ-carbide can contribute to the strength, microcracks preferred to initiate around κ-carbide, which is detrimental to the tensile properties. At the annealing temperature between 900 °C and 1200 °C, since the change of phase fractions is very moderate, the grain sizes, instead of phase fraction change, have a significant effect on the tensile properties.

claim: In the comparison of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels (Table 2), the present Fe-10Mn-10Al-0.7C steel has the highest α phase fraction (40%), boundary κ, and no κ dispersed in the γ matrix, resulting in the lowest ultimate elongation (36.6%); increasing C content may compensate for the loss of strength due to reduced Mn content.
evidence_text: Considering the highest α phase fraction in the present steel among the steels, the boundary κ-carbide between some large α grains, and the inhomogeneous microstructure, it is understandable why the ultimate elongation of the present steel is lowest among the steels. There is nearly no κ-carbide dispersed in the γ matrix of the present steel... Since Mn content in the present steel is designed to be low, the C content may need to be increased to improve the mechanical properties.
